original_abstract:
This review provides a comprehensive look at cosinor-based rhythmometry, a suite of regression-based statistical methods used to analyze time series in chronobiology. Unlike many traditional spectral analysis tools, the cosinor approach is highly versatile, permitting the analysis of non-equidistant data and the derivation of confidence intervals for rhythmic parameters such as MESOR, amplitude, and acrophase. 

The author explores the progression of the method from the "single cosinor"—initially designed for short, sparse data sets—to more advanced iterations like the "multiple-component cosinor" and the "population-mean cosinor," which allow for more complex waveform modeling and inferences across groups. The paper details the critical assumptions underlying these least-squares procedures, including normality, independence of residuals, and stationarity, while offering diagnostic strategies to address potential violations. Furthermore, the review discusses specialized extensions for non-stationary data, such as gliding spectral windows and chronobiologic serial sections, which track how rhythmic characteristics evolve over time. By incorporating practical examples ranging from circadian blood pressure monitoring to multi-year "chronomes," this review highlights how cosinor-based techniques remain essential for rhythm detection, parameter estimation, and the advancement of personalized chronomedicine.
humanized_abstract:
Chronobiology on the Web; Reviews of chronobiology and chronotherapeutics: VII This article is a general review of cosinor-based rhythmometry, a family of regression-based statistical methods widely used in the analysis of time sequence data in chronobiology. In contrast with the other, more restrictive methods generally derived from Fourier Transform (and thus essentially requiring equidistant data), cosinor methods can readily analyze non-equidistant data and allow the derivation of precise confidence intervals around any selected rhythm parameter(s), including MESOR, amplitude and acrophase.

This chapter describes the development of the method from its inception as the “single cosinor” for analyses of short periods of sparse data, to its more sophisticated extensions like the “multiple-component cosinor” and the “population-mean cosinor”. The important assumptions underlying the least-squares approach embodied in these methods are discussed, including the assumptions of normality of residuals, independence of residuals, and stationarity, together with possible approaches to diagnosing violations of these assumptions. In addition, extensions which deal with non-stationarity include those using gliding spectral windows and chronobiologic serial sections that capture the changes in the characteristics of rhythms as they evolve through time. Illustrative examples of the applicability of the methods are given from studies ranging from short term (24 hour) ambulatory blood pressure monitoring to analyses of “chronomes” that span periods of up to several years. The cosinor methods and their extensions continue to be of great value for rhythm detection, parameter estimation and for advancing the field of chronomedicine that aims to provide a unique biological time characterisation of each individual, hence the term personalized chronomedicine.
